does: This paper introduces FinSafetyBench, a bilingual red-teaming benchmark for evaluating how safely large language models refuse harmful financial requests. The benchmark contains 14 subcategories covering financial crimes and ethical violations, grounded in real-world cases and compliance standards. The authors conduct extensive experiments across general-purpose and finance-specialized LLMs using three attack settings, systematically identifying vulnerabilities where adversarial prompts circumvent safety mechanisms. Their analysis reveals that models show greater susceptibility to jailbreaks in Chinese compared to English contexts, and demonstrates that prompt-level defenses alone prove insufficient against sophisticated, implicit manipulation strategies that exploit nuanced language and domain knowledge.
connects: This work extends existing AI safety research, particularly red-teaming methodologies and benchmark development, into the specialized domain of financial compliance. It builds on prior work evaluating LLM robustness against adversarial attacks and jailbreaking attempts, while responding to the critical gap between general-purpose safety evaluation and domain-specific risks in high-stakes applications. The research acknowledges established financial crime typologies and regulatory compliance standards, connecting to literature on responsible AI deployment in regulated industries. The bilingual focus addresses growing recognition that safety evaluations in non-English languages reveal distinct vulnerabilities, extending findings from earlier cross-lingual AI safety studies.
enables: This benchmark establishes a foundation for developing more robust financial compliance mechanisms in LLMs, motivating research into defense strategies specifically calibrated for financial contexts rather than generic prompt-level fixes. The identified vulnerabilities around implicit manipulation and language-specific attacks point toward better architectural defenses and multi-layered safety approaches. Future work could leverage FinSafetyBench to train compliance-aware models, develop domain-specific alignment techniques, and create adaptive safeguards that consider linguistic and cultural contexts. The benchmark also enables regulatory bodies and financial institutions to validate LLM safety before deployment, supporting responsible integration of language models into regulated financial services.
